title: Fractional Perfect Matchings in Hypergraphs
speaker: Andrzej Rucinski
affiliation: Adam Mickiewicz University in Polznan,  Poland; Emory University
abstract: A perfect matching in a k-uniform hypergraph H = (V, E) on n verticesis a set of n/k disjoint edges of H, while a fractional perfect matchingin H is a function w : E → [0, 1] such that for each v ∈ V we havee∋v w(e) = 1. Given n ≥ 3 and 3 ≤ k ≤ n, let m be the smallestinteger such that whenever the minimum vertex degree in H satisfiesδ(H) ≥ m then H contains a perfect matching, and let m∗ be definedanalogously with respect to fractional perfect matchings. Clearly, m∗ ≤m.

proof_stages.label: Dirac-type matching-threshold setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines k-uniform hypergraphs, perfect matchings, d-degrees and minimum d-degree thresholds, motivates the problem from Dirac-type Hamilton-cycle results, and introduces the extremal parameter m_d(k,n) together with graph, edge-count, and codegree benchmark cases.

proof_stages.label: Two lower-bound barriers and the main integer conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Parity and space-barrier constructions give two competing lower bounds, leading to the conjecture that the perfect-matching threshold is asymptotically the larger of them, with the unresolved range concentrated at small d<k/2.

proof_stages.label: Absorption framework for perfect matchings
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains the absorbing method: construct a small matching able to absorb leftover k-tuples, find an almost-perfect matching in the remaining hypergraph, and then combine them to obtain a perfect matching.

proof_stages.label: Fractional perfect matchings and Farkas duality
proof_stages.summary: Fractional perfect matchings are defined as nonnegative edge weights summing to one at every vertex, and Farkas' lemma converts nonexistence into a vertex-weight certificate whose edge sums are nonnegative but whose total sum is negative.

proof_stages.label: Fractional-to-integral threshold theorem
proof_stages.summary: A fractional threshold m_d^*(k,n) is introduced, the space barrier is shown to lower-bound it, and the main reduction theorem states that the integral threshold is asymptotically controlled by the maximum of the half-barrier and the fractional threshold.

proof_stages.label: Probabilistic proof of the reduction theorem
proof_stages.summary: Using an absorbing lemma and the Frankl–Rödl almost-perfect matching theorem, the proof randomly samples many vertex sets and then samples edges according to fractional perfect-matching weights to build an almost-regular low-codegree subhypergraph, obtains an almost-perfect matching, and lets the absorber finish.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of the fractional conjecture to fractional Erdős matching
proof_stages.summary: The attempt to prove the fractional threshold conjecture is reduced via threshold hypergraphs, weight normalization, link counting, and LP duality to a fractional Erdős matching-type edge-count conjecture, from which the speaker extracts known cases and the new m_2(5,n) consequence.

examples.name: The case k=3, d=1 giving the 5/9 threshold
examples.note: The speaker plugs k=3 and d=1 into the space-barrier coefficient to show concretely why the predicted vertex-degree threshold is 5/9, matching the earlier Hamilton-cycle discussion.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the displayed lower-bound formula to k=3,d=1 and identifies the resulting coefficient as 5/9

examples.name: The case d=2, k=5 where the half-barrier still dominates
examples.note: This numerical case illustrates that even below k/2 the maximum in the conjectured threshold can still be the parity half-barrier rather than the space barrier.
examples.engagement_reason: he plugs in d=2,k=5 to get the 61/125 barrier and compares it with 1/2 to say the half-barrier wins

passes.reason: The d=2,k=5 case is a threshold edge case where the half/parity barrier remains the extremal obstruction, refuting the naive converse that d<k/2 means the size barrier dominates.
the d=2,k=5 instance is controlled by the parity half-barrier, a tight lower-bound obstruction to any sub-half guarantee

examples.name: Jump in pair-degree thresholds for almost-perfect versus perfect matchings
examples.note: For delta_2 the speaker contrasts the roughly s threshold for matchings of size s<n/k with the n/2 perfect-matching threshold, illustrating a discontinuity in the highest-degree setting.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the pair-degree thresholds n/2 versus roughly n/3 for perfect versus almost-perfect matchings as a phenomenon, without working it out

passes.reason: The pair-degree jump is a critical-threshold phenomenon used to show that the smooth almost-perfect-to-perfect behavior from the vertex-degree case fails.

examples.name: One-uniform and graph cases of the weighted lemma
examples.note: The l=1 case has a one-line proof and the l=2 graph case follows from the simple form of fractional vertex covers in graphs, making the weighted conjecture concrete in low uniformities.
examples.engagement_reason: he discusses proving the weighted lemma in the l=1 and l=2 cases, including a one-line proof for one-uniform hypergraphs and a graph argument via vertex covers

passes.reason: The one-uniform and graph cases are low-uniformity base cases of the weighted lemma, with the one-uniform case especially collapsed to singletons.

examples.name: s=2 as Erdős–Ko–Rado
examples.note: The speaker identifies the s=2 endpoint of the matching extremal conjecture with the Erdős–Ko–Rado theorem, situating the final conjecture among familiar extremal results.
examples.engagement_reason: he only identifies the s=2 case of the matching conjecture as Erdős–Ko–Rado

passes.reason: The s=2 case is the minimal nontrivial matching-number case and becomes the Erdős–Ko–Rado maximum-size intersecting-family theorem.
s=2 is the minimal nontrivial matching case and reduces to the extremal Erdős–Ko–Rado intersecting-family theorem
The case s=2 is the minimal nontrivial matching-number case and is exactly the extremal Erdős–Ko–Rado theorem.

the s=2 case is the minimal nontrivial matching endpoint and is exactly the extremal Erdős–Ko–Rado intersecting-family bound
